"Shock Horror (A)" - along with 170 other short musical cues! - was released on an album entitled "The Editor's Companion, 3/4" in 1984. text:
No Funney First of all, allow me to say that was a very unnecessary insult.  Second of all, you as a Japanese should understand very well how there are different accents, or branches, within many languages.  Japanese has several, with both the conflicting Tokyo and Hiroshima accents as prime examples. American, British, Irish, Scottish, etc. are all accents, or branches, of the English language.

Many of the major languages in the world have different "branches", and each one has differences not just in sound, but spelling as well, or words can have different meaning.  For example, the meaning of "mate" in American English is just as it would be in a dictionary, however the very same word in British, Irish, etc. can mean friend; this coming from a person who has several friends from the UK.

Point being, it is not ignorant at all to say a word in English can be different depending on what form of the language it is.  And just to prove the point further, if you look in an English dictionary, BOTH forms of the word, "spelled" and "spelt", can be seen, meaning both are officially accepted terms for the pass-tense form of the word "spell".

If anything, YOU were the ignorant one here.
And btw (by the way), here is the link to the dictionary definition to "spell":
http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/spell?s=t
Note the two forms of the word, "spelled" and "spelt" at below in bold letters.
